data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Erica Furfaro and Margaret Vitrano

Stabilizing Growth Setup Through Volatility

Market Overview

The outbreak of war in the Middle East accelerated a selloff among growth stocks that started with software weakness and resulted in wide losses for the first quarter. The S&P 500 Index declined 4.3% while large cap growth stocks were the worst-performing segment of the market, with the benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index falling 9.8% compared to a gain of 2.1% for the Russell 1000 Value Index.

Growth stocks were pressured as investors more pointedly questioned the return on investment from massive capital spending on AI buildouts as well as the viability of application software business models amid new generative AI tools introduced during the quarter. Rising bond yields resulting from the inflationary pressures of higher oil prices due to the U.S. and Israeli conflict with Iran also weighed on higher-multiple growth stocks.

Against this volatile backdrop, the ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Strategy outperformed its benchmark as we saw increased market dispersion away from the technology sector and momentum stocks that led the market in 2025. Materials was the largest contributor to relative performance in the quarter as industrial gases provider Linde (LIN) and paint and coatings maker Sherwin-Williams were solid performers in an environment that has begun to reward companies with quality fundamentals.

The Strategy also saw contributions from a diversified mix of industrials names, led by Eaton (ETN), a key provider of equipment to enable electrical connectivity; W.W. Grainger (GWW), a distributor of industrial supplies; and RTX (RTX), a defense contractor. We were also encouraged by the rebound in Netflix (NFLX) after the streaming provider withdrew from a bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD); we believe the fundamental setup for its business remains robust.

These holdings helped offset weakness among the portfolio’s software names. Tax prep software maker Intuit (INTU), credit score provider Fair Isaac (FICO), cloud hyperscaler Oracle (ORCL) and enterprise software firm Salesforce (CRM) were all impacted by a broad acceleration in the selloff among software companies feared to be disintermediated by AI. Fair Isaac, known for its FICO score, was also hurt by concerns about growing competition that we believe are overblown as the company continues to deliver considerable value to its customers. We completed an exit from Salesforce this quarter after trimming the position as part of our actions to reduce software exposure over the past year.

Since 2022, Salesforce has experienced slowing growth in its core business across its sales and service cloud segments and, more recently, marketing cloud. While Salesforce is executing on several product strategies to accelerate growth, we think these initiatives are not substantial enough to bend the curve on overall revenue growth. Product execution on innovation strategies has also been mixed. We believe Salesforce will continue to face rising competitive and model transition pressures as the software-as-a-service ecosystem moves from seat-based to usage-based pricing.

Portfolio Positioning

We continue to be optimistic on trends in the semiconductor sector and added a new position during the quarter in Texas Instruments (TXN), a company focused on analog semiconductor devices and embedded processing. The company sells products with long life cycles to a diversified customer base and has a unique free cash flow story. While the company’s cash flow has been depressed by sluggish demand and elevated capex, we believe cash flow can inflect meaningfully as both of those trends reverse. We also view Texas Instruments as well positioned for an improving manufacturing outlook as 30%–40% of its customer base is in industrials (Exhibit 1).

Exhibit 1: Sector Positioning vs Benchmark

We also added two new positions in the health care sector with the additions of Roche (RHHBY) and Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY). These moves brought the portfolio’s health care exposure back to a slight overweight versus the benchmark and continued our efforts to improve quality of our health care holdings. We believe Roche has an attractive growth runway with multiple positive Phase III readouts in early-stage breast cancer and multiple sclerosis. These should enable Roche to grow revenues through the loss of exclusivity period for several of its current cancer and multiple sclerosis drugs and secure its place among pharmaceutical companies that can grow revenue for the next decade.

Alnylam has pioneered RNAi drugs that work to "silence" certain proteins created by the body that can malfunction and cause disease. The company’s key product, Amvuttra, treats a condition that leads to protein misfolding in the heart and, eventually, heart failure (cardiomyopathy). While Amvuttra entered the cardiomyopathy market to strong early success in 2025, missed expectations heading into 2026 provided a chance to buy the stock at valuation levels we think incorporate conservative assumptions for its commercial opportunity and very little value for any other pipeline drugs. Alnylam also reached free cash flow breakeven in 2025, a milestone that we seek in biotech investments to derisk some downside potential.

We exited PayPal (PYPL) and Equinix (EQIX) during the quarter. When we invest in businesses like PayPal executing upon turnarounds, we regularly weigh the positive outcome of improved execution with downside risks, including the opportunity cost of longer than expected paths to improvement. While there are some signs of improvement in PayPal’s business, the turnaround has proved more challenging than expected, leading us to exit the position.

Equinix owns and operates interconnected data centers that provide network and cloud platforms for enterprise customers. The stock was sold as the company remains in the middle of a large capex investment cycle while AI tailwinds from increased customer spending on inference will take time to show through.

Outlook

We believe the start of 2026 demonstrated stabilization for the Strategy compared to the prior year. While the top-heavy composition of the Russell 1000 Growth Index causes us to maintain a significant weighting to the Magnificent Seven, our mega cap exposure did not have an overriding influence on first-quarter performance. Instead, we were encouraged by the diversified contributions the Strategy received across multiple sectors outside IT.

Rising geopolitical risks also highlighted our ability as active managers to oversee the level of risk in the portfolio. As market volatility rose during the quarter with the Iran conflict, we continued to dial back risk by trimming strong recent performers to redeploy the proceeds into more attractive ideas. Looking ahead, some early signs of positive early-cycle momentum in industrials have eroded somewhat due to the conflict, yet we remain optimistic that pent-up activity in the manufacturing economy can be supportive of portfolio results.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Strategy outperformed its Russell 1000 Growth Index benchmark in the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy delivered positive contributions across two of the nine sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total). The primary contributor was the materials sector, while the IT and consumer discretionary sectors were the main detractors.

Relative to the benchmark, overall sector allocation contributed to performance. In particular, overweights to the industrials and materials sectors and stock selection in the communication services and materials sectors supported performance. Conversely, stock selection in industrials and health care detracted as did an underweight to consumer staples.

On an individual stock basis, the leading contributors to relative performance included ASML (ASML), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Eaton, Netflix and an underweight to Microsoft (MSFT). The primary detractors from relative returns were Intuit and Fair Isaac as well as not holding Costco Wholesale (COST), GE Vernova (GEV) and Lam Research (LRCX).

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we initiated a position in Blackstone (BX) in the financials sector and exited a position in Marvell Technology (MRVL) in the IT sector.

Erica Furfaro, Portfolio Manager

Margaret Vitrano, Portfolio Manager

data.title: AMASS Brands Aims For Direct Listing Despite Weak Financial Results
data.description:
AMASS Brands Is A Company In Transition

AMASS Brands Inc. (AMSS) has filed for a direct listing of its common stock, according to an S-1 registration statement.

The company markets bulk wine and spirits in the United States.

While management is moving to rationalize its portfolio, the industry is suffering from declining usage from younger demographics, the company has little liquidity, and the listing will provide no new resources.

Since the only form of meaningful new capital is potentially increasingly dilutive convertible financing, my outlook on the direct listing is to sell (avoid it).

What Does AMASS Do?

AMASS sells bulk wine through distributors and also increasingly offers non-alcoholic alternatives and spirits.

It also sells directly to the consumer and through international distributors through third-party partners.

The company's portfolio of brand categories includes:

  • AMASS - flagship spirits and non-alcoholic products.

  • Spirits - GEM&BOLT Mezcal, Calirosa Tequila.

  • Wine - Summer Water, Folly of the Beast and premium imports.

  • Non-alcoholic Wine - Good Twin.

  • Minority Interests - De Soi (non-alcoholic) and HpO (protein sparkling water).

A breakdown of the firm’s revenue contribution by segment is shown in the graphic below:

AMASS is led by founder, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Mark T. Lynn, who founded the company in 2019.

Mr. Lynn has been a director on the Board of Digital Brands Group (DBGI) and has extensive experience in the beverage and consumer industries.

Investors have invested fair market value of approximately $37.6 million to-date and include MVL, Streeterville Capital and individuals.

What Is The Primary Market For AMASS?

AMASS operates primarily in the U.S. wine market, where most of its revenues are derived.

According to a 2026 market research report by Grand View Research, the U.S. wine market was an estimated $75.3 billion in 2024 and was expected to exceed $157 billion by 2033.

If achieved, this would represent a Compound Annual Growth Rate [CAGR] of 8.8% from 2025 to 2033, a reasonably robust growth rate for an already large market and over a significant number of years.

The table wine product type accounted for 81.5% of the total market share in the U.S. in 2024, with the retail (off-trade) segment leading by distribution channel, at 76.6% of the market in 2024.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the U.S. wine market by product type, from 2023 to 2033:

The U.S. wine market is fragmented, but major competitors include:

  • E. & J. Gallo Winery.

  • Constellation Brands (STZ).

  • The Wine Group.

  • Trinchero Family Estates.

  • Pernod Ricard (PDRDF).

  • Deutsch Family Wine & Spirits.

  • Accolade Wines.

  • Castel Frères.

  • Casella Family Brands.

  • Bronco Wine Company.

  • Numerous others.

Niche markets are gaining share among discerning consumers, as evolving tastes continue to shape the U.S. wine industry.

Consumers want diverse wine experiences, valuing "authenticity, sustainability…driving demand for wines that align with these preferences."

However, younger demographics are drinking less alcohol and participation rates are declining, according to a 2025 Gallup study.

Younger demographic cohorts, such as younger millennials and older Gen Z persons, have a growing belief that moderate alcohol consumption is bad for their health, resulting in a record low 54% of Americans saying they consume alcohol.

AMASS’s strengths include its multi-category portfolio, design-led branding, alignment with younger and moderation-oriented buyers, and celebrity partnerships (Katy Perry, Adam Levine).

The company's weak points are execution strain across its various brands, reducing focus on some of its spirits in the short term, using direct-to-consumer for only brand support channel.

Opportunities for AMSS are through focusing on existing wholesalers for deeper distribution penetration, new product launches in the moderation and wellness segments, and cross-selling and portfolio bundling.

The firm is seeing threats due to demand softness in US spirits, large incumbent competitiveness and regulatory complexity.

Recent Financial Results For AMASS

AMASS’s financial results have produced falling topline revenue, higher gross profit and gross margin, worse operating losses but reduced cash flow from operations, as the table shows below:

The reason for the rising gross margin in 2025 was due to depressed gross margin in 2024 from an acquisition where the company exited loss-generating contracts.

The firm is also shifting toward higher-margin products and better inventory discipline.

So, in sum, AMASS stopped the gross margin losses but is still structurally unprofitable.

AMASS’s Direct Listing Plan

AMSS is registering for sale up to 12,432,021 shares of common stock held by shareholders.

In the prospectus, the company cites an Expected Reference Price of $16.00 per share, which if confirmed by the firm’s Advisor on the day of listing, would mean an initial market capitalization of approximately $199 million.

Certain shareholders have agreed to a tiered lock-up period, subject to certain exceptions.

Management intends to host an "investor day," with content similar to that of a traditional IPO roadshow presentation.

The company will receive no proceeds from the direct listing process, so it will have no additional financial resources with which to further its position in the markets it operates in.

The firm said it is not currently a party to any legal claims or proceedings that it considers material.

However, it is involved in a legal action against a former landlord in a lease termination case. The parties have entered into a termination and settlement agreement.

The sole Financial Advisor on the direct listing is Maxim Group LLC.

The table below shows various financial metrics for the firm:

(Source - SEC.)

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of the end of 2025, AMASS had $824,962 in cash and equivalents and $25.6 million in total liabilities, of which $5.9 million was debt.

Net free cash flow for 2025 was negative $1.9 million.

Working capital at the end of 2025 was a negative $8.2 million, a weak liquidity indicator for an asset-light consumer brand company.

Also, the direct listing doesn’t provide any proceeds to repair the balance sheet and negative stockholders’ equity.

Using its trailing twelve-month operating cash flow loss, the company has a cash runway of only a few months, and its accounting firm has issued a "going concern" opinion.

AMASS Is An Unprofitable Consumer Company

AMASS has produced declining top line revenue, but improving gross profit and worsening losses.

The firm is burning cash at a quickly unsustainable rate, and a direct listing doesn’t really help the company other than providing liquidity for existing investors and perhaps enticing a potentially new investor.

While the company is trying to rationalize its portfolio mix, it is under liquidity pressure, making the listing highly speculative.

Management’s growth strategy is to expand distribution, scale its DTC efforts, launch new products and pursue acquisitions.

But where the firm is going to get the resources to pursue its goals is a mystery.

The market for its products is also under pressure, with declining usage and participation rates across younger demographics, which are the future for consumer brands.

The firm is seeking to focus more on moderation-oriented consumers, which is all to the good, but its gross profit base is too small in relation to its commercial and overhead structure.

As to risks for investors, the company is claiming "emerging growth company" and "smaller reporting company" designations, which allow management to produce substantially less information for shareholders.

Many of these company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO.

The firm also sold its AMASS trademark and it now depends on a licensed mark it doesn’t own.

AMASS also had material weaknesses in its internal control over financial reporting, namely:

Specifically, we lacked a comprehensive and formalized accounting and financial reporting policies and procedures manual addressing items such as closing procedures, descriptions of responsibilities, segregation of duties, management review procedures and the Company’s accounting policies.

(Source - SEC.)

Additionally, investor Streeterville Capital has purchased rights to Series C convertible equity, but as a variable price convertible with downside protection, protecting Streeterville but putting in place the potential for an increasing dilution effect, although it is limited to a 10% ceiling on Streeterville’s position.

My overall summary of the AMASS direct listing is:

  • Positive - Adjusting its brand portfolio strategy.

  • Uncertain - Ability to execute on growth strategies.

  • Negative - Industry decline, poor liquidity, Streeterville investment terms, material financial reporting weaknesses.

So, while management is taking steps to correct its portfolio mix, the company is operating on thin liquidity in an industry under pressure, with the only source of capital in the form of convertible financing that may have the characteristics of being increasingly dilutive to shareholders.

My outlook on the direct listing is therefore to Sell (avoid it).

Expected Direct Listing Date: To be determined.

data.title: Davenport Small Cap Focus Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Q1 2026 Market Review

The Small Cap Focus Fund (DSCPX) finished the first quarter down 0.92%. This trailed the 0.89% gain for the Russell 2000® Index. While Q1 results were muted in an absolute sense, the path was quite tumultuous. To start the year, the strategy fell behind the index as many of the market dynamics that made 2025 challenging persisted. However, mid-way through the quarter, we experienced a material shift as more speculative pockets cooled and improving economic sentiment gave way to a rally in traditional businesses with more cyclical profiles. On the heels of this rotation, the Fund was slightly ahead of the Russell heading into the month of March. Unfortunately, the rapid onset of conflict in the middle east unwound much of this momentum, causing us to lag modestly. We note this progression because we believe it illustrates and confirms various assertions we made in our year-end letter regarding the types of market conditions that would allow our approach to shine. While we are frustrated with the way the quarter ended, we remain confident in the Fund's positioning and encouraged about the future.

Fund Update

Contributors: California Resources Corp (CRC) was the Fund's top contributor during the period, advancing more than 50% as oil prices surged alongside the Iran conflict. We elected to trim our position materially into strength. Elsewhere, we saw explosive gains out of Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC) and Cognex Corp (CGNX), which experienced gains north of 40% and 35%, respectively. Here again, we elected to trim positions into strength, but maintain exposure given constructive long-term outlooks.

Detractors: Key detractors for the period included Stubhub Holdings, Inc. (STUB), Verra Mobility Corp (VRRM) and Janus International Group, Inc. (JBI). Each of these companies reported lackluster Q4 results and gave outlooks that we thought should have been largely anticipated given well-known macro factors. Nonetheless, each stock experienced dramatic drawdowns that were compounded by the general risk-off tone as the conflict intensified towards the end of the quarter.

Fund Activity

We continued to roll with the punches during the volatile first quarter, responding to volatility by trimming and adding to positions in opportunistic fashion. Following the severe drawdown mentioned above, we added to our position in Verra Mobility Corp (VRRM). Outside of this activity, we initiated a few new positions that might bore the average day-trader, but are quite exciting to us at current levels. These are Cohen and Steers, Inc. (CNS) and Rayonier, Inc. (RYN). Each are tied to the Real Estate Investment Trust (REIT) space, which has undergone weakness alongside cyclical fears and adverse interest rate movements. From a big picture perspective, we are attracted to the massive disparity between public and private market valuations across the sector. Further, in a world fraught with concerns around AI disruption and rapid change, we like the durability and tangibility of real assets – a feature we believe investors will increasingly come to appreciate. Cohen and Steers is the leading asset manager in the REIT space. The company has a fortress balance sheet, leading fund performance and should exhibit meaningful operating leverage if and when investors view public REITS more favorably.

Rayonier is a timber REIT and is the country's second largest landowner with just over 4 million acres. The stock is down sharply from highs as a moribund housing market has weighed on demand for lumber. At this price, we think depressed demand for wood products is adequately discounted and view a housing recovery as a free call option. RYN now trades at an implied valuation of roughly $1,900 per acre. This is a steep discount to private market values as recent timberland transactions have taken place closer to $3,000/acre. Looking at it differently, one could buy over two acres of timberland for less than the price of an ounce of gold. Moreover, RYN has been selling HBU real estate (generally for development) and realizing 5-15x valuation uplifts compared to its current per acre valuation. We also note timber has historically been a very good store of value and inflation hedge. Right now, we are paying a deeply depressed price for a hard asset and essentially zero for the company's wood products business. Meanwhile, we are being paid a 5% dividend yield while we wait.

Conclusion

In sum, we caught a glimpse into what a different backdrop might look like for the Fund, and remain constructive on our positioning. With incremental funds, we have elected to lean into high quality situations, with real asset values and business models that are difficult or near impossible to disrupt.

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®, Co-manager, Small Cap Focus Fund

Christopher G. Pearson, CFA®, Co-manager, Small Cap Focus Fund

data.title: CoreWeave: Hypergrowth Is Here, But The Cash Cost Is Massive (Downgrade)
data.description:
In December 2025, I reiterated my "Strong Buy" rating for CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), calling the share price decline overdone as the market reacted to a slip in quarterly sales, while essentially the indication was that sales slipped from one quarter into the next. Since then, the stock price has risen more than 30%, outperforming the S&P 500, which remained flat. However, it should be noted that the stock is still down about 12% since I initiated coverage. So, from my first "Strong Buy" rating until now, the stock has a lot to recover while the market is seemingly getting riskier.

GDP And Inflation Will Be A Watch Item On CoreWeave Buildout

The big opportunity is, of course, the growing capacity that is being deployed. With revenues of around $12.5 billion expected at the midpoint for 2026 with 1.7 GW capacity deployed, that would signal close to $10 billion in revenues per GW, keeping in mind a gradual increase in deployment throughout the year by simply averaging the targeted year-end capacity and the capacity at the end of 2025. AI demand is still growing, so the opportunity is evident.

However, there also are risks. The war in Iran is eroding GDP growth outlooks, and that may also reset AI CapEx down the chain. I do not expect the hyperscalers to throttle back on AI spending, but down the chain in small businesses, I do expect businesses to rethink their AI strategies. That is not expected to be a material fallout, but I view AI as a GDP multiplier in a procyclical way. So, when economies are growing, the AI investment and return on that investment are higher. When GDP declines, the gains the AI investments may bring are smaller, and companies rethink capital allocation. Now, there is some in padding in the sense that companies that are already using AI are not going to drastically change their AI deployment, but it is important to keep in mind that GDP growth will be a watch item, as I believe AI’s strength lies in opportunities of acting as a growth multiplier rather than a cost-cutting mechanism.

In the supply chain, we do note that CoreWeave’s Q4 capital expenditures were higher than the company had expected because CoreWeave put capacity in service faster than expected, as discussed during the earnings call:

Turning to capital expenditures. CapEx in Q4 totaled $8.2 billion and $14.9 billion for the full year, higher than anticipated due to our team's ability to put infrastructure in service ahead of our expectations. As we previewed last quarter, the meaningful growth in construction in progress in Q4 to $9.4 billion, an increase of $2.5 billion quarter-over-quarter reflects the significant scale of infrastructure we are on track to deliver in the near term.

I wouldn’t say that fully shifts back the capacity deployment bottleneck from data center infrastructure back to GPUs, as both are key watch items, but it does show the company is executing well. Currently, we are seeing older GPU family prices rising again, and that points at continued demand exceeding supply.

With the war in Iran, inflation is also rising, and that will become more important to keep an eye on. It means that construction of new data center infrastructure may become more expensive, and interest rates may not decline as fast as hoped, making the cost of new debt higher than previously anticipated and potentially, on variable debt, resulting in higher interest costs.

CoreWeave Earnings Show Scaling Pressure On Margins

For the full year, CoreWeave’s revenues climbed 168% to $5.1 billion, with adjusted operating income climbing to $666 million, indicating a 13% margin. A year ago the adjusted operating margin was 13%. It should be noted that as the company accelerates its scaling, there is some margin pressure as the company absorbs costs first before the revenues start flowing. Adjusted EBITDA hit $3.1 billion at a 60% margin compared to $1.2 billion at a 64% margin in 2024. So, whenever the CapEx deployment is not a smooth one, we will also see substantial operating margin pressure, and that is something that investors need to keep in mind. The company ended the year with a $66.8 billion backlog compared to $15.1 billion in 2024.

For 2026, the company expects $12-$13 billion in revenues, with CapEx rising to $30-$35 billion. Margins remain pressured, but mature margins would be 20%-30%. The run-rate revenues would be $17-$19 billion exiting 2026 and over $30 billion exiting 2027.

We do note that the company has put some debt facilities in place to cover the capital expenditures:

  1. An $8.5 billion draw-term loan allowing for an initial borrowing of $7.5 billion and another $1 billion conditioned on asset stabilization.
  2. A $1.75 billion upsized offering of notes.
  3. An upsized $3.5 billion plus 500 million convertible note offering.

So, $14.25 billion in CapEx is covered through the notes aligning closely with the expected increase in CapEx this year. We also see demand continuing to rise with a new agreement with Anthropic, with revenues expected to start later this year; a $21 billion deal with Meta; and an agreement with Perplexity.

CoreWeave Is Still A "Buy"

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For CoreWeave, I have a "Buy" rating. That used to be a "Strong Buy" rating, but as I tweaked our scoring system that the rating is based on, it is now a "Buy." Expected sales growth is 90% per year through 2028, with nearly 100% EBITDA growth per year. Growth is not the main issue for the company. The main challenge is the CapEx, which will grow to north of $30 billion, while operating cash flows will only hit $17.5 billion by 2028. The company has arranged additional debt facilities, but I am also implementing capital raises of $14.5 billion this year, $8.5 billion next year, and $7 billion in the year after in combination with debt increases of $25.5 billion. What is going to determine the share price trajectory is the CapEx development and operating cash flow growth and how the CapEx will be financed. My price target is now $157, indicating 42% upside.

As the company grows, we should see margins increase substantially. This year will be a dip because the capacity that is coming online will result in many costs to be added while full revenue generation follows a ramp-up schedule. As said, I have modeled capital raises to fund part of the capital expenditures, with the remainder being raised via debt markets. As the business scales, free cash flow remains negative, but we note that the net debt leverage is improving.

Conclusion: CoreWeave Still Has Upside, But We Watch Cost Inflation And Financing

I believe that CoreWeave definitely has upside. In the current market setting, I do not expect demand to slow down, as growth is mostly driven by long-term agreements with hyperscalers. However, smaller businesses will likely carefully look at their AI budgets. For CoreWeave, the supply chain in GPUs and data center infrastructure is a watch item in combination with inflation and the interest rate environment. I still am bullish on CoreWeave, and I think the business is being managed reasonably, but we are also seeing global uncertainty increase, which may have consequences for construction costs and cost of debt.

data.title: Energy Transfer: A Blend Of Income And Growth I Continue To Buy Into
data.description:
Returns Are Starting To Compound

The first time I covered Energy Transfer LP (ET) was back in late November 2025, and the returns have been strong. Since then, the units have returned a total of 17.96%, compared to the S&P 500's (SP500) return of just 1.36%. Of course, a measure of this short of a time span isn’t really fair all the time. But I think ET will be able to continue to outperform over the long term due to the structural tailwinds I pointed out back then now starting to materialize.

Data center energy demand is set to grow about 15% per year until 2030. This will have a knock-on effect for gas production in North America

I pointed out how energy demand will grow at an accelerated pace in the US because of data centers. The deal with Cloudburst is just one of many I think will come in the short-term for ET. Since November 2025, ET has also started to deliver gas to one of the mega projects that Oracle (ORCL) is doing right now in Texas.

Because of the rise in the unit price, the yield has fallen somewhat. When my first article came out, the yield was sitting at 7.97% TTM and has since come down to now 6.98% on an FWD basis. But most estimates suggest that ET will raise the distributions per unit from $1.37 to $1.48 by 2028, showing an 8% growth rate. Trends are positive, and the thesis is still very much in play. I’m reiterating my Strong Buy for the stock.

Commanding Premium Pricing In A Demanding Market

Most who are familiar with ET already know about the wide footprint that it has, but also that it’s one of the more integrated midstream companies in the Permian region.

Just to also cover what the company is and does, there are 4 main parts that make up ET. Interstate and Intrastate gas transportation. Then you have midstream operations like gathering and processing. On top of this, ET also works with NGL and refined products and crude oil as well, covering all from gathering to storing it. Because of the rise in oil production in the Permian Basin, the knock-on effect has been increased gas transportation demand as well, given that it's a by-product. Because of the diversified business model, ET is able to benefit from both.

So far, there have been 4 major announcements where Energy Transfer is looking to be the main supplier of gas for large-scale data centers. Expectations are that U.S. gas demand could grow upwards of 25 Bcf/d by 2030. For reference, the current average consumption sits at 92 Bcf/d. A 27.1% increase over a 4-year period is quite substantial, even more so for an industry like this. It’s not like software, where the scaling can be done with little cost sometimes. Here there need to be actual pipelines built, upstream facilities, and much more.

And for full year 2025, we spent approximately $4.5 billion on organic growth capital, primarily in the NGL and refined products

During the last earnings call by ET, management mentioned $4.5 billion was spent as growth capital. In the March investor presentation, though, guidance for 2026 sits at $5-5.5 billion in growth capital expenditures. The 22.2% increase on the higher end is again substantial. But it’s essential, seeing as because of ET’s footprint and aggressive investments, it’s been able to secure about 7-8% of the future gas demand in the U.S. By this I’m referencing the 1.9 Bcf/d figure following the 4 major supply deals. I expect more to come in the following years, consolidating ET’s growth and market share.

6 Bcf/d in contracting translates to $25 billion in transportation fees, spread out over 18 years. Dissecting this, I can assume that 1.9Bcf/d equals $7.9 billion in guaranteed revenues. Because it’s then also spread out over 18 years, it implies an average firm fee of $0.63/Mcf.

The quote there is from the first article I made on ET. I was looking at the locked-in revenues for ET, the revenue obligations. There are 2 segments we need to look at here, the Interstate and Intrastate, the latter of which covers the Texas region.

During FY2025, ET reported $2.445 billion in revenues for the first segment and $891 million in transportation fees for the latter. This comes out to $0.37 per Mcf and $0.17 per Mcf, seeing as the volumes were 18,018 BBtu/d and 13,946 BBtu/d. The point here is that the supply deals that ET is right now signing are sitting at a 2-3x premium compared to what it normally commands in terms of transportation rates per Mcf. So data centers are not just volume growth, much of which ET already has secured; they are also a serious margin expansion for the business.

Just to clarify, the interstate revenues are using the segment margin, as that is the relevant figure here. With consistent adj. EBITDA margins of about 80%, I can see the bottom-line impact of the new data center deals. At $0.63 per mcf, the annual mcf supply is 584 million mcf. With the premium pricing of $0.63 per mcf, the revenue addition is $367.9 million. By then applying the 80% EBITDA margin, ET would see $294.3 million in pure EBITDA contributions from this. This would represent 9.2% of the current EBITDA from the two segments.

This tells the story of how ET is no longer just an income play but also a growth play as it consolidates market shares and builds more earnings available for distributions. Going back to what I noted at the start about the outlook for the distributions, growing it by 8% until 2028 seems very possible at this point.

Partner distributions were $4.725 billion in 2025 and $6.459 billion combined with non-controlling interest payouts as well. An 8% increase puts this at $6.975 billion, leaving a gap of $516 million. The 4 data center deals fill 57% of this on their own. But because ET then only pays out 63.6% of operating cash flows and 40.4% of consolidated adjusted EBITDA, I think there is no doubt that ET can continue to be a serious income play on top of this volume growth we are seeing. It even has the means to accelerate the distribution payouts, in my opinion.

Valuation

The EV of ET sits at $153 billion right now, and based on the 2026 guidance, the same adjusted EBITDA as in 2025, the EV/EBITDA multiple is just 9.18 ($153 billion / $17.65 billion). This is obviously at a premium to the sector median of 6.75x and ET’s own 5-year average of 7.87x. What justifies this is the growth and the premium pricing we are seeing ET command right now. It comes back to the margin expansion and not just the sheer volume growth.

Compared to its peers, ET is really not trading at any premium. Rather, it’s pretty severely undervalued. Perhaps this comes from the market eyeing more growth in the others; ET is, after all, not the only MLP that will benefit from the increase in data center energy demand. But still, the picture tells me that buying at around 7% yields two things. First, a strong income, of course, but secondly, the chance of a re-valuation as the price corrects to be more in line with peers.

Even if that doesn't come, the downside protection is pretty substantial here too. ET is one of the largest MLPs in the U.S. Operating over 20 Bcf/d means that its market share is over 20% of the average U.S. demand (92 Bcf/d). This combined is why a Strong Buy is the only rating that makes sense for it right now.

Risks

One of the main areas bears tend to point to is the debt pile that ET has, which currently sits at $68.3 billion. Current maturities are small at just $25 million, though. The LT figure is in combination with both Energy Transfer and Sunoco LP, which on its own has $13.3 billion. When the debts actually start to be more relevant is after 2030, it seems. Up until then, it's between $500 million and $1.5 billion in maturities annually. With the amount of adj. EBITDA that ET generates, I see no issue in paying this back.

Still though, the ballooning debt is putting pressure on the bottom line, as the interest expenses have risen to $3.4 billion TTM, up from $2 billion in 2022. Measured against the total debt, the cost has gone from 4.09% to 4.8%. This is only natural, though, as the interest rate has risen during the same period. It’s still down from 2024, when the cost of debt was 5.1%. The ratio of total debt to the 2026 EBITDA guidance is 4.26x ($70.094 billion / $16.450 billion).

Pro forma for a full year of acquisitions, Energy Transfer’s leverage ratios are now in the lower half of its 4.0-4.5x target range

This puts it well within what it is aiming for in terms of leverage. When this becomes a risk is when the Fed might start to signal interest rate hikes, which is a real possibility this or next year if inflation starts to spike, much in part because of companies no longer able to stomach the tariffs or the rising fuel prices because of the ongoing conflicts in the Middle East.

The Full Picture

Energy Transfer is one of my favorite MLPs not just because of the income play that it is but because of the downside protection, seeing as it trades at a discount to peers yet shows serious growth potential. The returns have been solid since I initiated coverage on the stock, and I see this continuing in 2026 and beyond. I’m watching out for how ET is now able to command premium pricing for its pipeline deals with data centers. This alone could accelerate the adj. EBITDA growth by a wide margin. I’m not purely looking at volume growth but also margin expansion. Once this starts to show up in the financial reports in the second half of this year or at the start of 2027, the upside might be lower than it is today. Rating ET units a Strong Buy.

data.title: PJT Partners: Go-To Recession Resistant Advisory Play Trades In Line With Exposed Peers
data.description:
PJT Partners (PJT) is a great firm. Restructuring continued to make headway in the previous results. Park Hill, the placement and secondary business, also continues to make records despite challenging comps last year, with secondaries becoming an interesting asset class for allocators in their own right. We were worried about a Park Hill slowdown in our previous coverage. Advisory also did well.

Since the results, the Iran War began, which is increasing costs of capital and has already dented deal volumes significantly. The fall-through of ceasefire talks means the hostilities will continue for the time being, with the conflict possibly escalating on the blockade, and transits already aborted halting a momentary resumption of volumes. The hoped-for sponsor recovery, talked up on account of the rate cuts finally having come in 2025, may not continue to materialize. Moreover, hopes pinned on equity markets to support other corporate finance activity may also be harmed due to the onset of the Iran War. The SaaS-pocalypse has also been a challenge for the advisory industry in general. But in the case of all these issues, while advisory might see Q1 pressure, restructuring could actually benefit from Iran War pressures on the economy and on financial options for over-leveraged companies, and some of the factors that led to strong Park Hill performances could also be persisting as sponsors once again back off from coming back to market. The SaaS-pocalypse could also help advisory, as it already has in Q4.

PJT is always the go-to countercyclical large-ticket advisory play, and it's even lagged the most YTD despite its relative attractiveness in the current market. We're not sure we'd play it because it does trade at double the trailing earnings PE of Moelis (MC), but it's more defensive than similarly discounted options since the onset of the war in terms of price performance.

Results and What's Next

The company continues to make partner investments, with partner headcounts growing 12% in 2025. In Q4, revenues were up 12%; they were up 15% for the year, meaning not that much deceleration at all compared to peers, which saw more of a hit into Q4. There was some comp and non-comp expense lift as a proportion of sales, but net profit still rose as well by a few points on a diluted basis. The consistent performance reflects some of the features of the PJT profile. While not necessarily a recessionary environment, 2025 saw record restructuring levels. This was actually a bit idiosyncratic, as not all companies in the space managed to grow their restructuring franchises. Advisory was also quite strong thanks to rate cuts supporting debt markets and the decent situation in equity markets even supporting some IPOs. In turn, there was a venue for sponsors to release a couple of their investments, and a bit of churn came back to PE, which started to transact a bit again. However, their return to markets remains in the Goldilocks zone for PJT because they are still not turning over enough of their portfolios yet to meaningfully return capital to LPs; hence, the Park Hill business has been raking in record revenues from providing services to find new owners of LP stakes in the secondary market, more than offsetting slowness in primary placement and fundraising activities. The secondary market has been really active at other companies too, and PJT management made an interesting comment, which is that secondary stakes in funds might actually become an interesting asset class in their own right now that the market has become familiar with them.

there is an increasing realization of the attractiveness of the secondary opportunity from an investment perspective, the absence of a J-curve, the ability to invest alongside sponsors where there's continuity of management specific identification of the assets, a real track record of performance

Paul Taubman, CEO of PJT, Q4 Earnings

Park Hill knocked out a phenomenal quarter to drive growth. Restructuring was a record too, despite no real economic traumas, due to a return to more normalized rates after aberrational lows from the zero-rate environment and also some challenges to select tech companies getting disrupted and areas within healthcare still challenged by their respective financial market conditions.

And we are pretty clear in how we communicate to our investors; we had our record quarter in restructuring. Q4 was the best restructuring quarter we've ever had.

Paul Taubman

What's key to understand about the summary above is that everything depended on the rate cuts because that brought down costs of capital. Sponsors coming back a bit depended on rate cuts since LBOs require low rate leverage to work out, and also because it supports equity markets for IPOs, which also supports ECM. Lower rates also mean DCM activity too at banks. The cost of capital declines from Fed rate cuts also mean less gravity on multiples, meaning decent PE exits and no scary write-downs to ruin the performance of infrequently valued portfolios, and also more sellers willing to part with their companies at attractive multiples in line with what was possible before inflation kicked in the first time around. M&A also grew, and PJT is a large-ticket player not really in the mid-market. 2025 was the year of mega deals; many firms saw lower counts but higher revenues, reflecting that, inflecting on the highest level, changes in regulatory and antitrust stance, regulation, and geopolitics.

So our concern is simply that the Iran War is reversing the lower cost of capital situation, with CPI already up 3.3% and consumer sentiment affected by price questions foremost on their mind, meaning we could see an uptick in inflation expectations as well, which is the real danger for the Fed and will prevent cut relief, maybe even spurring a hike. This is bad for M&A, where Axios data already shows the pain caused by the Iran War. There were signs of this already at the time of the earnings call, with announced volumes for January down 10% YoY with intensifying geopolitical uncertainty. It is also bad for the sponsor business, where the whole narrative of their return depended on rate cuts. However, it's not bad for Park Hill if sponsors pull back again and secondaries become really needed again, and it's not bad for restructuring. With reference to restructuring:

in a multiyear period of elevated restructuring activity...

...historically low interest rates that were aberrational and we're dealing in a more normalized rate environment today...

...And the reality is we haven't really hit a recessionary environment for an extended period of time, if we were to, then all this commentary sort of gets taken off the board and you're looking at a meaningful leg up.

Paul Taubman

Bottom Line

PJT is a more steadfast advisory play that has a strong countercyclical character to it. With it not trading better than peers whose Q1 financials will be less mitigated by their respective restructuring franchises, we see PJT as a relatively tactical play which should log a resilient Q1 performance as opposed to an embattled one, which is in the cards for peers. But there are other ways to look at it where it may appear less attractive. PJT trades at 50x trailing earnings, while Moelis trades at 20x. PJT is the better pick at the moment, so the multiple likely reflects that. It also puts it at an objectively high level, one we think we can beat with some of the other picks we follow closely in more obscure parts of the global market, especially given that some PJT cylinders won't be firing in a quarter marred by a still ongoing Iran War and related reinflationary energy crisis.

data.title: XTL: Amazon For Globalstar Is Just The Latest Catalyst
data.description:
Major news struck the global telecom industry on April 14, 2026. Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) announced it intends to acquire Globalstar (GSAT) for $11.6 billion. Share of the target company soared 11% in the premarket, which should only add to gains in the State Street SPDR S&P Telecom ETF (XTL). GSAT is the largest holding in the ETF.

I have a buy rating on XTL. With market-leading momentum and a still-reasonable valuation, I expect the space megatrend to continue benefiting the small ETF. Today, I’ll assess the fundamentals, outline the valuation, and do a technical deep dive.

XTL: A Global Industry ETF Leader YoY

According to the issuer, XTL seeks to track a modified equal-weighted index, providing diversified exposure to the telecommunications sector across large, mid, and small-cap companies. This fund covers sub-industries such as wireless services, integrated telecommunications, and equipment providers, allowing investors to take targeted strategic or tactical positions.

XTL is a small ETF, with just $590 million in assets under management as of April 13, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while its trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at just 0.95%, about 0.2 percentage points below that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is pristine for XTL, currently an A+ according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. That strength helps XTL rank No. 1 out of 12 in its Sub Class and No. 27 out of 3701 overall on Seeking Alpha as the Q1 earnings season gets underway in earnest. But the fund is risky, given an elevated 28% annualized volatility figure and 41% turnover.

In terms of liquidity, average daily volume is low at under 60,000 shares, while XTL’s median 30-day bid/ask spread is a bit wide at 19 basis points. So, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 5-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. A large portion of the allocation is in mainly US small caps (60% along the bottom row of the style box). Just 3% of XTL is classified as large-cap growth, which is important, as that means the ETF may diverge significantly from the S&P 500’s performance.

On valuation, the current price-to-earnings ratio of 19.8x is incredibly attractive in light of the long-term earnings growth rate of 19.7%. The PEG ratio is just about 1.0x on the high-momentum product.

XTL: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, investors should not be surprised to see that XTL is almost equally comprised of Information Technology and Communication Services. What’s more, while Globalstar is the largest holding, it only commands a 3.9% stake. Other high-momentum equities such as Iridium Communications Inc. (IRDM), Ciena Corporation (CIEN), Viavi Solutions Inc. (VIAV), and Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) are currently atop the list.

Something I must underscore is that while the ETF name might sound like it holds blue-chip, high-yield telecom stocks, that couldn’t be further from the truth. Income investors with a low risk tolerance may not be drawn to this fund.

XTL: Holdings and Dividend Information

In terms of seasonality, we are very close to the start of the best part of the year.

The May through August stretch has produced materially positive returns in price data back to 2016.

XTL: Bullish Seasonality On Tap

The Technical Take

With shares looking good on valuation and the calendar appearing friendly, XTL’s technical situation is very strong. Notice in the chart below that the ETF has been trending higher since April 2015. A pullback occurred last October and November, but the bulls quickly reasserted their presence after a 16% Q4 drawdown. $160 is now key long-term support, but that seems a far way down from Tuesday’s closing price of $209.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Furthermore, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has ranged in a bullish zone between 40 and 80 for almost six months now. With the 50dma and an uptrend support line having confluence at $185, that’s the current bullish line in the sand.

The volume profile is also sanguine, thanks to a high amount of shares traded below the current all-time high price level.

XTL: Bullish Uptrend, Rising 200dma, High Volume, Strong RSI

The Bottom Line

Dealmaking is on the rise, and Amazon’s latest move underscores the demand for space-first plays. XTL is a way to play it, and the valuation & technicals support the thesis of more upside as the year progresses.

data.title: Ally Financial Q1 Earnings Preview: Annual Guidance Will Come Under Scrutiny
data.description:
Ally Financial Inc.'s (ALLY) upcoming Q1 release on April 17th is expected to be a routine exercise, with earnings surprises gradually coming down in recent quarters. The stock has been performing roughly in line with the financial sector, and it seems that broader macroeconomic factors are in the driver's seat when it comes to share price performance.

After a notable improvement in ALLY's return on capital and net interest margin in 2025, the current outlook for 2026 was slightly favorable when the company reported its full fiscal year results in late January. The net interest margin is expected to improve slightly within the range of 3.6% to 3.7%, up from 3.47% in 2025. Net charge-offs are expected to remain roughly in line with the prior year, driven by strong credit performance in the company's commercial portfolios.

Overall, the market seems to be prepared for a relatively quiet period with slight improvement in certain metrics. This is also reflected in ALLY's lower trading volume in recent months and the stabilization of the price-to-book multiple at around 1.

Events from recent weeks, however, now threaten to derail the already weak recovery that ALLY's management was expecting in 2026, and investors should be prepared for such a scenario.

Expect Margin Pressures

During the last conference call, ALLY's management provided the annual outlook on the assumption of 2 rate cuts in 2026 with the caveat that NIM is expected to experience some pressures in Q1. Deposit repricing and higher yields on newly originated loans were initially anticipated to provide a boost for margins in 2026.

We expect full year NIM between 3.6% and 3.7%. The range for NIM reflects the evolving path of interest rates as the Fed easing cycle continues with 2 cuts assumed for 2026. As we have consistently messaged, we are liability sensitive over the medium term and asset sensitive in the very near term. We'd expect early beta to drive a relatively flat margin through 1Q. But given current trends on lease residuals, we expect NIM to be slightly down on a sequential basis.

Source: ALLY Q4 2025 Earnings Transcript (emphasis added).

However, since then the expectations of rate cuts in 2026 have disappeared, and the market is now pricing in a stable federal funds rate through late 2027.

As a result, the expected deposit repricing is now less likely to have a positive impact on ALLY profitability. With long-term yields remaining relatively stable, this has now caused the yield curve to flatten, which could potentially turn into an unexpected headwind for ALLY's margins in 2026.

While it might be too early for any adjustments in the company's full fiscal year guidance, such a scenario now appears more likely than just a few months back.

On the asset side, originated yield fell in the fourth quarter of 2025, and as long-term yields remained under pressure in Q1, it would appear that NIM is highly likely to be below expectations in the upcoming quarter.

The good news is that in spite of certain red flags regarding the state of the U.S. consumer, overall demand for auto loans remains strong in the first months of 2026, and this could partially offset the negative impact on margins.

Bear In Mind The Multiple

Given the somewhat mixed macroeconomic environment and changes to interest rate expectations, it should come as no surprise that ALLY remains conservatively priced with a P/B multiple of below 1. As we could see on the graph below, in the first couple of months of 2026, the stock experienced significant multiple contraction that was dictated by the broader decline of equities in the financial sector.

A potential margin headwind through the rest of 2026 is not necessarily reflected within that multiple, as we could infer from the long-term relationship between ALLY's price-to-book multiple and the company's core return on tangible common equity (ROTCE).

Core ROTCE stood at 11.1% in Q4 of 2025, and the current P/B multiple is still above the long-term trend-line - even after accounting for the share price drop in recent months.

* P/B multiple is based on the adjusted tangible book value per share (Adjusted TBVPS).

** Q4 2025 multiple is based on ALLY's current share price.

Thus, it appears that the market is pricing in an increase in Core ROTCE in 2026, which is in line with the guidance provided during the last quarterly report.

Investor Takeaway

Ally Financial's upcoming Q1 report is largely expected to come in line with the company's previously provided guidance. In spite of the share price drop in recent months, the stock's price/book multiple does not suggest any deviation in investors' expectations from the management outlook during the Q4 2025 report. This could turn sour for investors later this week, as recent movements in short-term rates could provide a headwind for the company's profitability. Overall lending in the automotive sector remains robust, and this could give investors some respite, but the overall guidance for 2026 is likely to come under scrutiny.

data.title: ClearBridge Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Evan Bauman, Aram Green and Amanda Leithe, CFA

Balance and Discipline Guide Growth Through Volatility

Market Overview

Surging volatility pressured U.S. equities to start 2026, as rapidly evolving macro conditions and the outbreak of a military conflict in the Middle East contributed to sharp shifts in investor sentiment. While volatility had already been building since late 2025 — driven in part by elevated valuations across growth equities beginning to compress — conditions became more unsettled as the quarter progressed. A broad-based sell off among more liquid, larger cap holdings put pressure on the Russell 3000 Growth Index, which declined 9.5% for the quarter, while small and mid cap growth categories held up relatively better, with the Russell Midcap Growth and Russell 2000 Growth indexes declining 6.3% and 2.8%, respectively.

Attacks by the U.S. and Israel on Iran in early March introduced an additional layer of uncertainty, particularly around the potential for disruptions to global oil supply and the resulting concerns over the inflationary impact that could arise from a prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Importantly, market behavior during the quarter was driven more by macro forces than company-specific fundamentals. Periods of indiscriminate selling impacted both cyclical and defensive sectors, creating dislocations often disconnected from underlying business performance. This environment reinforced the importance of maintaining a disciplined, bottom-up investment approach while avoiding overreliance on macro forecasting.

Portfolio Performance

The ClearBridge Growth Strategy modestly underperformed its Russell Midcap Growth benchmark during the first quarter, giving back a small portion of the strong relative performance achieved over the past several quarters. Performance was pressured primarily by stock selection within the information technology (IT) and financials sectors, while strength in areas such as communication services and historically underrepresented industrials and consumer discretionary sectors underscored the benefits of our multi-year portfolio diversification efforts.

"This market reinforces the importance of a disciplined, bottom-up approach while avoiding overreliance on macro forecasting."

Within IT, a broad-based selloff in software and higher-multiple growth names weighed on results. Despite solid quarterly results, holdings such as Autodesk, Shopify (SHOP) and CrowdStrike (CRWD) declined as investors questioned growth durability and valuation levels across the subsector. AppLovin (APP), which operates a software platform for advertisers to enhance the marketing and monetization of their content — particularly within mobile apps — was also a notable detractor, on fears of AI disruption and rising competition from Meta. We see AppLovin as an early adopter and longer-term beneficiary of AI, as greater gaming and application development should increase the need for discovery. We also believe recent competitive concerns are overblown as the company has successfully competed against Meta for years and there are no signs it is being displaced, particularly as it relates to AppLovin’s higher-value impressions.

In financials, Robinhood Markets (HOOD) detracted from performance due to a pullback in retail trading volumes and bitcoin pricing following a strong 2025. While near-term cryptocurrency trends have moderated, we continue to see long-term potential in the platform’s ability to expand its ecosystem and capture share across a broader set of financial services offerings.

These pressures were partially offset by strength in other areas of the portfolio. In industrials, Vertiv (VRT), a manufacturer of power and precision cooling systems, continued to benefit from the buildout of data center and AI infrastructure, while defense contractor L3Harris (LHX) delivered solid performance amid sustained global defense spending trends. Relatively new addition XPO (XPO), the fourth-largest less-than-truckload carrier in North America, continued to benefit from initiatives under its new leadership to improve service levels, pricing discipline and margins.

Holdings in the communication services and consumer discretionary sectors were also key contributors, led by Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS), which is exploring a separation of the New York Knicks and Rangers into two standalone public companies, enhancing strategic flexibility, and TJX (TJX), which put up better than expected same-store sales growth despite a more constrained consumer spending environment.

Portfolio Positioning

While our core investment philosophy remains unchanged, the portfolio today reflects a broader and more balanced expression of growth than in the past. A foundation of steady compounders continues to provide stability and downside support, while a complementary set of disruptive growth businesses enhances the portfolio’s ability to participate effectively across a wide range of market environments.

Within technology, we have been deliberate in refining exposures, particularly across software. What was a more significant relative overweight to our benchmark last year has been reduced, reflecting both valuation discipline and a more selective approach to identifying long-term winners in an evolving landscape shaped by AI. Our exit from HubSpot (HUBS) is representative of this discipline: while the company maintains a strong competitive position in the mid-market customer relationship management space, rising concerns around AI-driven disintermediation, particularly in the less complex and more price-sensitive small and medium business segment of the market, led us to reallocate capital toward higher-conviction opportunities.

At the same time, we have used market volatility to increase the quality of the portfolio, adding businesses with strong competitive advantages and attractive long-term growth profiles. In industrials, we initiated a position in Fastenal, a high-quality compounder with a differentiated onsite distribution model and structural advantages in scale and supply chain. We believe the company is well-positioned as a share gainer in a fragmented market to drive durable growth through the business cycle, offering offensive characteristics in the event of an industrial recovery as well as the ability to outgrow end markets amid a weaker environment. In communication services, we initiated a position in Spotify, a global audio streaming platform with more than 750 million monthly active users. We were attracted to Spotify’s durable, subscription-led growth and improving pricing power and profitability. We took advantage of the recent selloff to initiate a position.

Finally, in consumer discretionary, we added Viking Holdings (VIK), a leading operator in river and luxury cruises. The company’s focus on a more affluent customer base, combined with strong pricing power and high returns on invested capital, positions it well to benefit from continued growth in experiential travel demand. We also initiated a position in Wayfair (W), where we see significant long-term opportunity as e-commerce penetration increases within the home furnishings category. The company’s asset-light model and logistics infrastructure provide a competitive advantage and should allow for meaningful margin leverages as revenue scales, particularly if housing activity and big-ticket spending normalizes over time.

We have also capitalized on recent market volatility to optimize our exposures to existing holdings. For example we increased our allocation to XPO, reflecting a preference for companies with both self-help initiatives and cyclical upside, particularly in an environment where macro conditions remain uncertain, as well as to fast-casual restaurant CAVA (CAVA), which continues to expand its location footprint and explore new menu innovations as it transitions from a newly public company to a large-scale sustainable growth franchise.

Outlook

Our outlook remains consistent: while macro uncertainty and volatility are likely to persist and may even increase as geopolitical developments evolve, these environments often create the most attractive opportunities for active, bottom-up investors. We continue to emphasize balance — owning companies with both offensive growth potential and defensive characteristics, including strong balance sheets, durable cash flows and capable management teams. This approach is designed to support performance across a wide range of market environments, rather than relying on a single factor or theme.

Importantly, we remain active. We are not standing still in the face of volatility but are instead using it to refine the portfolio — trimming positions where risk-reward has become less favorable and adding to high-conviction ideas where valuations have become more compelling. We also believe the ongoing broadening of market leadership, particularly beyond mega cap technology, is a constructive development for our investment universe. After a prolonged period of concentration, a more balanced market should reward differentiated, high active share portfolios such as ours.

Ultimately, we are confident that the Strategy’s improved diversification, disciplined process and focus on high-quality growth businesses position it well to navigate current conditions and continue delivering long-term value for clients.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Growth Strategy underperformed its Russell Midcap Growth Index during the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy had positive contributions from two of the nine sectors in which it was invested. The contributors were the industrials and materials sectors, while the IT sector detracted the most.

On a relative basis, overall sector allocation detracted from performance, while stock selection contributed. Stock selection in the IT, health care and financials sectors, overweights to the IT and communication services sectors and a lack of exposure to the energy sector weighed on performance. Conversely, stock selection in the communication services, consumer discretionary and industrials sectors, an overweight to the materials sector and an underweight to the financials sector proved beneficial.

On an individual stock basis, the biggest contributors to relative returns were L3Harris Technologies, Madison Square Garden Sports, Vertiv, Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) and Johnson Controls International (JCI). The largest detractors from relative returns were AppLovin and Shopify and not owning Comfort Systems (FIX), Quanta Services (PWR) and Targa Resources (TRGP).

In addition to the transactions listed above, the Strategy exited positions in Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) in the consumer discretionary sector and Pinterest in the communication services sector.

Evan Bauman, Portfolio Manager

Aram Green, Portfolio Manager

Amanda Leithe, CFA, Portfolio Manager
